pages.text: The effect of superplasticisers on the workability and compressive strength of concrete made with ﬁne recycled concrete aggregates P. Pereira a, L. Evangelista b, J. de Brito a,⇑ a Instituto Superior Técnico, Av. Rovisco Pais, 1049-001 Lisboa, Portugal b Instituto Superior de Engenharia de Lisboa, R. Conselheiro Emídio Navarro, 1, 1959-001 Lisboa, Portugal article info Article history: Received 7 June 2011 Received in revised form 4 October 2011 Accepted 5 October 2011 Available online 29 November 2011 Keywords: Recycled aggregates Superplasticisers Concrete Compressive strength abstract The reuse of structural concrete elements to produce new concrete aggregates is accepted as an alterna- tive to dumping them and is favourable to the sustainability of natural reserves. Even though the con- struction sector is familiar with the use of coarse recycled concrete aggregates, the recycled concrete ﬁnes are classiﬁed as less noble resources. This research sets out to limit the disadvantages associated with the performance of concrete containing ﬁne recycled concrete aggregates through the use of super- plasticisers. Two types of latest generation superplasticisers were used that differ in terms of water reduction capacity and robustness, and the workability, density and compressive strength of each of the compositions analysed were then compared: a reference concrete, with no plasticisers, and concrete mixes with the superplasticisers. For each concrete family mixes with 0%, 10%, 30%, 50% and 100% replacement ratios of ﬁne natural aggregates (FNA) by ﬁne recycled concrete aggregates (FRA) were analysed. Concrete with incorporation of recycled aggregates was found to have poorer relative performance. The mechanical performance of concrete with recycled aggregates and superplasticisers was generally supe- rior to that of the reference concrete with no admixtures and of conventional concrete with lower performance superplasticisers. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction 1.1. Preliminary remarks The use of recycled aggregates (RA) is an important step forward in the development of a more sustainable society. Using RA in concrete is an alternative to dumping it and also helps the sustainability of natural aggregates’ reserves. Early research focused mostly on processing concrete waste, its size grading, mechanical characteristics and durability aspects [1,2]. The mechanical performance and economic aspects related to the use of recycled aggregates concrete (RC) have also been ana- lysed recently [3]. Notwithstanding this initial reticence, more re- cent literature surveys do not suggest that the use of RA in structural concrete is inappropriate. More recent research on concrete made with coarse recycled concrete aggregates [4–6] and ﬁne recycled concrete aggregates (FRA) [7] has led to positive results concerning the use of RA in structural concrete. Hansen’s review [2] on including FRA in concrete reveals some contradictory results among the ﬁrst experimental programmes. Even though the results generally indicate reductions in strength of up to 40%, as in Soshiroda’s work [8], other research, such as that by Rasheeduzzafar and Khan [9], did not ﬁnd signiﬁcant differ- ences when they incorporated FRA in the concrete mix. Even though the use of ﬁne recycled aggregates (FRA) in con- crete tends to be associated with adverse effects on its compressive strength, Leite [10] notes that its use in structural concrete may be beneﬁci

compressive strength is not affected by the incorporation of RA up to a 30% replacement ratio of FNA with FRA. 1.2. Scope and methodology of the investigation The characteristics of RA, the concrete composition and the type of superplasticiser used markedly inﬂuence the mechanical perfor- mance of RC. Even though there are various studies on the inﬂuence of the two ﬁrst parameters very little is known about the interaction between superplasticisers and RA. Therefore this paper aims to analyse the inﬂuence of FRA on the mechanical per- formance of concrete as well as the effect of superplasticisers on the properties of this modiﬁed concrete. Superplasticisers have various effects on concrete, such as increasing its rupture stress, allowing a lower cement content while maintaining strength and workability, increasing workability with the same water and cement contents, and decreasing permeability [13]. The dispersion mechanism of the new generation of superplasticisers, polycarb- oxylic esters, is particularly efﬁcient thanks to two types of repul- sive forces between the cement particles: electrostatic repulsion linked to the presence of a negative charge caused by the carbox- ylic group, and steric repulsions characteristic of long polymer chains on the aggregate’s surface. This dual effect allows reduc- tions of up to 40% in mixing water [14]. The experimental programme started with the production of an original concrete (OC) from a ready-mixed concrete plant that was moulded in the laboratory. This OC was used to produce the recy- cled aggregates so that the component materials and their main characteristics would be known and their production controlled. Once crushed, the ﬁne materials were sorted by size fraction to al- low an exact replacement of natural sand, i.e. the size distribution of the FRA was adjusted to match that of the FNA. Concrete mixes without superplasticisers (WS), with regular superplasticiser (SP1) and with high performance superplasticiser (SP2) were produced and ﬁve mixes with different FRA incorporation ratios were then made for each of these concrete types. In order to establish a com- parative basis for the various mixes they were tested in fresh state for their slump and density. Keeping the slump constant for all concrete mixes studied was chosen as the levelling parameter. After curing, the concrete mixes were tested in the hardened state for compressive strength and stress–strain diagrams were drawn. The experimental results were then analysed in detail and correlations were established between the properties of ﬁne recy- cled concrete aggregates concrete (FRC), the properties of FRA, the replacement ratios of FNA by FRA and the superplasticiser used. 2. Experimental programme 2.1. Original concrete (OC) The OC was produced in a ready-mixed concrete plant and poured into moulds in the laboratory, with ambient curing conditions. Its composition is typical of a commercial concrete, designed to correspond to a C30/37.X0 (P).S3.Cl0.40.Dmax25 according to NP EN 206-1. The composition of the OC is provided in Table 1 . The slump and 28-day average compressive strength (in 150 mm cubes) were 120 mm and 37.3 MPa, respectively. 2.2. FRA production After a 28 day curing period the OC blocks were crushed using a jaw-crusher. Properties such as the ﬁnes percentage and maximum aggregate size (smallest sieve through which at least 90% of the aggregate’s mass could pass), which are relevant in the production of FNA, we

3.2. Speciﬁc density of concrete The results presented in Fig. 5 and, in relative terms in Fig. 6 , show that the incorporation of FRA, which has lower particle den- sity than FNA, leads to a decrease in the concrete’s density. In line with other authors [11,30,31] who have found linear relationships with good correlation ( Fig. 7 ) between concrete density and FRA incorporation ratio, our study also obtained high correlation fac- tors (R2 above 0.95) (Fig. 6) but with non-linear relationships. Since the regression line of the SP2 family had the smaller slope, followed very closely by the superplasticiser-free family and then by the SP1 family ( Fig. 6 ), it indicates that when SP2 was used the greater compacity of concrete somehow offset the relative in- crease in water/cement ratio due to the incorporation of FRA. In the FRC made with SP1 the higher water/cement ratio was more inﬂuential. The differences are quite small, however. 3.3. Compressive strength The average compressive strength ( fcm) and standard deviation (r), measured at 7, 28 and 56 days for all FCRA incorporation ratios and superplasticisers, are presented in Table 5 . It also gives the variations found as a function of the FCRA incorporation ratio (DFRA), for each superplasticiser family, and as a function of the superplasticiser type ( DSP), for each FCRA incorporation ratio. Figs. 8–10 shows the variations in compressive strength with the FCRA incorporation ratio, as well as the standard deviation for each point, while Fig. 11 shows the correlation between the 28-day compressive strength of the various FRCs and their effective water/cement ratio. After 28 days curing all FRCs showed a decrease in compressive strength due to the incorporation of FRA, with ﬁgures of 4.8%, 15.4% and 3.3% for the WS, SP1 and SP2 families, respectively. These reductions are insigniﬁcant for the superplasticiser-free and SP2 concrete mixes and it can even be said that the compres- sive strength remains approximately constant. The addition of superplasticiser in the mixes led to compressive strength gains of up to 34.8% and 69.5% for the SP1 and SP2 families respectively. Constants k1, k2 and k3 have been determined empirically, through the nonlinear regression model proposed, and are valid for the concrete mixes in this study. fc ¼ k1=k ðw=cÞef 2 /C1ð 1 /C0 k3 /C1 W24 /C1 rÞð 2Þ Based on the results obtained these parameters were deter- mined, k1 = 230.3, k2 = 25.9 and k3 = /C0 0.077, corresponding to R2 = 0.96. Fig. 11 shows the graphic representation of the equation proposed coupled with the actual ﬁgures obtained in the tests. The equation shows that the inﬂuence of the incorporation of FRA on compressive strength is much smaller than the inﬂuence of chang- ing the w/c ratio. Fig. 12 indicates that the inﬂuence of the superplasticisers increases with shorter curing times. This is related to the dispersant capacity of the superplasticisers that were used, which allow a much quicker cement hydration that rises with their rela- tive efﬁciency. The general trend of the lines in Figs. 13–15 , for 7, 28 and 56 days of curing, indicates that concrete’s sensitivity to the presence of FRA increases with age. In fact, the relative strength losses grow for every mix with FRA as the test specimens age, com- pared with the concrete without these aggregates. It seems that the favourable effects of the superplasticisers tend to fade as the concrete matures. The determination of stress/strain constitutive laws of the con- crete mixes studied did not reveal behavioural differences caused by the incorporation of FRA. However, the use of superplasticisers did lead to an increase in yielding stress and a reduction of the yielding path length, typical characteristics of a high-performance concrete. Figs. 16–18 show the stress/strain constitutive laws of the concrete mixes tested, grouped by content and type of Fig. 11. 2. The greater compacity of the mix provided by the plasticisers may prevail over the effect of the higher water/cement ratio in the determination of the speciﬁc density of FRC; 3. Compressive strength gains occurred in FRC, and these were greater the higher the water reduction capacity of the superplasticiser; 4. The strength gains from using superplasticisers are intrinsi- cally associated with the reduction of the water/cement ratio; 5. Concrete mixes made with a regular superplasticiser suf- fered bigger relative compressive strength losses than those made with a high-performance superplasticiser that proved to be more robust in the presence of FRA; 6. The effect of superplasticisers on compressive strength is the more pronounced the lower the FRA incorporation in the mix; 7. The r–e curves suggest that concrete made with FRA has a similar behaviour to conventional concrete of the same com- pressive strength. Acknowledgement The authors are grateful for the support of the ICIST Research Institute, IST, Technical University of Lisbon and of the FCT (Foun- dation for Science and Technology). of the superplasticiser, due to a reduction in the water/cement ratio. The results for the incorporation of FRA are in agreement with previous studies [7] and this good performance may be ex- plained by: (i) a higher un-hydrated cement content in the mix [32] providing additional hydraulicity [33]; (ii) the fact that the interfacial transition zone (ITZ) between the FRA and the cement paste may have beneﬁted from the porosity of FRA having decreased locally, with the w/c ratio allowing a more effective ﬁll- ing in by the hydrated products [34], and ﬁnally (iii) a ﬁller effect linked to the smaller size of FRA compared with FNA [35], but only for small replacement ratios (between 10% and 30%). A combina- tion of these three phenomena can overcome the repercussions of the lower strength of FRA, thus justifying these results. With higher replacement ratios the effect of the lower mechanical char- acteristics of FRA overrides the combined effect of these phenom- ena. FRC made with SP1 show a more drastic reduction in compressive strength with increased incorporation of FRA than the other mixes, since the increase in the effective w/c ratio was also bigger. It is widely recognised that there is a close relationship between concrete compressive strength and the w/c ratio. Neville [36], cit- ing Abrams’ work from the early 20th century, reports that Eq. (1) provides a good correlation between these two parameters, where k1 and k2 are empirical constants. Nixon [1] reports that the compressive strength of RC is directly linked to increasing the w/c ratio as required to correct workability issues. fc ¼ k1=k ðw=cÞ 2 ð1Þ Bearing these principles in mind, an empirical Eq. superplasticiser. For RC0 the yielding path is particularly conspic- uous. However, for RC1 this path almost vanished as the maximum stress increased, and for RC2 these trends are even more marked, leading to a rupture that may be considered as fragile. The phe- nomenon of ductility loss as the compressive strength increases is well known [37] and is associated with the brisker propagation of cracking as the compressive strength goes up. The evolution of stress with strain shows that there is an earlier loss of stiffness in mixes with FRA. This is because the AFR paste is more fragile, which facilitates cracking propagation [38] and is directly linked with the lower compressive strength. The similar path of the curves agrees with earlier research [39]. Fig. 19 shows the gains of the RC and FRC made with superplas- ticisers over the plasticiser-free mixes that have the same FNA/FRA replacement ratio. The 28-day compressive strength gains attribut- able to the superplasticisers were higher for RC than for FRC, though the difference is slight. This can be justiﬁed by the increase in speciﬁc surface of FRA in the mix for the same superplasticiser content, given the fact that RA are longer and more angular than NA. 4. Conclusions The experimental programme enabled several conclusions to be drawn in relation to the use of superplasticisers in concrete made with ﬁne recycled aggregates. In some instances FRC show worse mechanical performance than the reference concrete mixes (RC). However, in every situation analysed it was possible to produce FRC of good enough quality for structural use. The following con- clusions can be drawn from the experimental results: 1. The efﬁciency of superplasticisers in terms of workability falls as the FRA incorporation ratio rises; 0.75 0.80 0.85 0.90 0.95 1.00 1.05 0.00 0.20 0.40 0.60 0.80 1.00 Relative compressive strength - fcm,28 / fcm,28,RC WS SP1 SP2 Replacement ratio -r Fig. 14. superplasticiser 2. The production of two concrete families with superplasticisers required the design of two more reference concrete mixes (RC1 and RC2) to supple- ment the superplasticiser-free reference concrete (RC0). The new mixes contained no FRA. The superplasticiser content of RC1 and RC2 was 1% of cement mass and, in order to keep slump within the speciﬁed range of 120 ± 10 mm, the water/cement ratio was decreased to offset the water reduction effect of the superplasticisers. 2.5. Concrete mixes’ composition Based on the Faury concrete design [21] ﬁve concrete mixes were produced for each superplasticiser used and for the concrete without superplasticisers: a refer- ence concrete (RC) and four RCs with FNA/RA replacement ratios of 10%, 30%, 50% and 100%. Every mix has the same effective aggregates size distribution and cement content. The lower water content implied a higher aggregates content so that the cement and superplasticiser content and the proportion of the aggregate fractions could be kept constant. Table 4 summarises the composition of the mixes. The con- crete mixes without FRA are RC0-superplasticiser-free, RC1-superplasticiser 1, and RC2-superplasticiser 2. For each family the other mixes are designated C0, C1 and C2, followed by the FNA/FRA percentage replacement ratio. 2.6. Concrete mixes with FRA The recycled concrete aggregates have a signiﬁcant water absorption capacity in a concrete mix. The simple volume replacement of NA by RA affects concrete work- ability and performance. For the mixing period of 10 minutes suggested by Evangel- ista and de Brito [22] it was necessary to study the water absorption of the FRA. The process devised by Leite [10] (Fig. 2) was adopted and it was found that after 10 min the FRA had absorbed 50% of its potential capacity. Therefore, in order to compen- sate for the FRA’s water absorption during mixing, water amounting to the difference between the maximum quantity taken in 10 min and that already within the FRA before mixing (it is not oven-dried) was added to the mix. The water/ce- ment ratios of the various mixes were also corrected to obtain the target slump, bearing in mind the increased internal friction of the mixes with FRA. The ﬁnal mixes’ composition is presented in Table 4 . The specimens were cured in a humid chamber as speciﬁed in NP EN 12390-2 [23]. 2.7. Tests on concrete mixes Fresh concrete was tested for workability and speciﬁc density according to NP EN 12350-2 [24] and NP EN 12350-6 [25], respectively. Seven, 28 and 56-day com- pressive strength was evaluated in 150 mm cubes, in accordance with NP EN 12390-3 [26]. Three, 5 and 3 specimens respectively were tested at each age. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Workability Fig. 3 presents the results of the Abrams cone slump test for all FNA/FRA replacement ratios. It shows that all mixes met the target workability apart from C0, 100 (concrete without superplasticiser and 100% replacement of FNA by FRA). The lower slump achieved for this particular case means that the optimal water/cement ratio should be slightly higher than that used. The performance of this mix therefore tends to deviate from that of the other mixes. Fig. 4 shows the relationship of the effective water/cement ratios between concrete mixes with superplasticisers and those without. It also shows the water reduction capacity of the super- plasticisers as a function of the replacement ratio of NFA by RCFA. Fig. 4 shows a linear relationship between t
